model_narrative: We choose which of a set of items to select and we set two nonnegative continuous quantities for each of several rows. There is also one overall nonnegative ceiling quantity that sits above every per-row continuous quantity. The objective is to make that overall ceiling quantity as small as possible.

sets.name: rows
sets.doc: the set of coupling rows; each row pairs two continuous quantities with a weighted combination of the on/off decisions

sets.name: bins
sets.doc: the set of on/off items, each of which is either selected or not

params.name: rhs
params.doc: a single fixed target value that every coupling row must meet exactly; a scalar

params.name: coef
params.doc: the contribution that selecting an item adds to a given row, given for every row and item combination

vars.name: xmax
vars.doc: a single nonnegative quantity that serves as a common ceiling over all the per-row continuous quantities; this is the value the model drives down

vars.name: xlo
vars.doc: the first nonnegative continuous quantity associated with each row

vars.name: xhi
vars.doc: the second nonnegative continuous quantity associated with each row

vars.name: b
vars.doc: the selection decision for each item; equals 1 if the item is selected and 0 otherwise

constraints.name: xmax
constraints.intent: The overall ceiling quantity must sit at or above the first continuous quantity of every row. For each row, the ceiling quantity must be at least as large as that row's first continuous quantity.

constraints.name: xmax
constraints.intent: The overall ceiling quantity must also sit at or above the second continuous quantity of every row. For each row, the ceiling quantity must be at least as large as that row's second continuous quantity.

constraints.name: xlo[r]
constraints.intent: Each row must exactly meet its fixed target. For every row, take the row's first continuous quantity, subtract its second continuous quantity, and add up the contributions of all the items that are selected, where each selected item adds its contribution for that row. This combined amount must equal the fixed target.

query: Engineering is pitching a redundancy floor on the first three switches: at least two of b33, b34 and b35 should come up together so the lead bank never runs on a single switch. Plug that in and tell me where the bottleneck lands.

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep the first coupling row balanced the other way round, so its low-side flow has to come out at least as large as its high-side flow. What does the optimizer return for the bottleneck under that tie?

query: We're looking at a leaner build that holds the count of active switches down to no more than thirty percent of the full switch bank. Run it and walk me through what the bottleneck becomes.
expected_pyomo: model.cap_bins = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[j] for j in model.bins) <= 0.3 * len(model.bins))

query: The board wants to see a uniform orientation across every coupling row, where each row's high-side flow is required to be at least as large as its low-side flow. Apply that across all rows and tell me the resulting bottleneck.

query: Let's try a redundancy package together: bring at least half the switch bank online, and on top of that keep the second coupling row's low-side flow at least as large as its high-side flow. What does the optimizer return for the bottleneck with both in place?
expected_pyomo:
model.rich_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[j] for j in model.bins) >= 0.5 * len(model.bins))
model.balance_r1 = Constraint(expr=model.xlo['r1'] >= model.xhi['r1'])

query: The plan leaves more than half the switch bank dark while the bottleneck sits where it does. Wouldn't lighting up at least fifty-five percent of the switches give the coupling rows far more room to flatten it out? What's the model seeing that keeps it running so sparse?
expected_pyomo: model.lean_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[j] for j in model.bins) >= 0.55 * len(model.bins))

query: Across the coupling rows the low-side flows look lopsided next to the high-side ones. Shouldn't each row carry its low-side flow at least as large as its high-side flow so the whole thing reads consistently? Walk me through what's pulling it the other way.

query: This configuration is carrying a lot of active switches for what it buys us. Couldn't we just run it far tighter and hold the active count to no more than a fifth of the bank? What's the tradeoff the optimizer is making by spreading across so many?
expected_pyomo: model.tight_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[j] for j in model.bins) <= 0.2 * len(model.bins))

query: It looks odd that b33 is committed while b34 stays off. Shouldn't b33 only be allowed on when b34 is also on, so the lead pair stays linked? What's keeping the optimizer from coupling them that way?
